title: CALM LAB
CALM LAB
Jane Doe
text:
Jane Doe

Graduate Student


Investigating sustained attention, mind wandering, and contemplative approaches to cognitive well-being.








Degree Program
Ph.D. Psychological Sciences


Contact
jane@university.edu


In the Lab
2024-Present








Research Interests
This is a longer paragraph style explanation of my research interests into something I don’t know how to do this but here we go, yipee kaya. Then this is a part of it as well. And what if it keeps on going on for a longer and longer and longer ezplanation. But does not stop here yet.

About
Jane Doe is a graduate student in the CALM Lab studying how attention fluctuates over time and how contemplative practices may help support cognitive performance and well-being. Her research focuses on understanding the dynamics of sustained attention—why focus is sometimes stable and at other times drifts—and how these fluctuations shape thought, behavior, and performance. By combining behavioral experiments with neurocognitive approaches, she investigates how attention operates both in everyday contexts and in situations that demand sustained mental effort.
In her research, Jane is particularly interested in the relationship between mind wandering and cognitive control. She studies how internal thoughts emerge during ongoing tasks and how individuals differ in their ability to maintain goal-directed focus. Her work draws on behavioral tasks and physiological measures such as EEG to examine the moment-to-moment dynamics of attention and the neural systems that support these processes.
More broadly, Jane is interested in how contemplative practices such as mindfulness may influence attention and emotional well-being. She is motivated by the possibility that training attention can enhance both cognitive performance and quality of life. Outside the lab, Jane enjoys exploring nature, reading, and practicing mindfulness meditation—interests that often inspire new questions about the mind and its capacity for focus and reflection.





 Back to top

title: Jason S. Tsukahara, Ph.D.
text:
Jason S. Tsukahara, Ph.D.

Principal Investigator


Dr. Tsukahara directs the CALM Lab and studies attention control across contexts of life and mind. His research integrates cognitive psychology, neuroscience, and contemplative science to understand the nature and potentials of attention.








Degree Program
Ph.D. Psychological Sciences


Contact
jane@university.edu


In the Lab
2024-Present








Research Interests
This is a longer paragraph style explanation of my research interests into something I don’t know how to do this but here we go, yipee kaya. Then this is a part of it as well. And what if it keeps on going on for a longer and longer and longer ezplanation. But does not stop here yet.

About
Jane Doe is a graduate student in the CALM Lab studying how attention fluctuates over time and how contemplative practices may help support cognitive performance and well-being. Her research focuses on understanding the dynamics of sustained attention—why focus is sometimes stable and at other times drifts—and how these fluctuations shape thought, behavior, and performance. By combining behavioral experiments with neurocognitive approaches, she investigates how attention operates both in everyday contexts and in situations that demand sustained mental effort.
In her research, Jane is particularly interested in the relationship between mind wandering and cognitive control. She studies how internal thoughts emerge during ongoing tasks and how individuals differ in their ability to maintain goal-directed focus. Her work draws on behavioral tasks and physiological measures such as EEG to examine the moment-to-moment dynamics of attention and the neural systems that support these processes.
More broadly, Jane is interested in how contemplative practices such as mindfulness may influence attention and emotional well-being. She is motivated by the possibility that training attention can enhance both cognitive performance and quality of life. Outside the lab, Jane enjoys exploring nature, reading, and practicing mindfulness meditation—interests that often inspire new questions about the mind and its capacity for focus and reflection.

title: Projects
text:
PROJECTS


Research areas and ongoing lines of work in the CALM Lab.

title: Projects
text:
{#measurement-individual-differences} Measurement & Individual Differences

This area focuses on how attention control can be measured reliably and how differences across individuals shape performance across a wide range of cognitive tasks and real-world outcomes.



Attention Control Measurement
Developing reliable and valid tools for assessing attention control across task paradigms and populations.

Ongoing Behavioral Tasks Psychometrics



Sustained Attention and Performance Variability
Studying fluctuations in sustained attention and how they relate to response variability, mind wandering, and task performance.

Ongoing Behavioral Tasks Individual Differences



Attention Control, Working Memory, and Intelligence
Examining the relationship between attention control and broader cognitive abilities, including working memory capacity and fluid intelligence.

Ongoing Latent Variable Models

title: Projects
text:
{#neural-mechanisms} Neural Mechanisms

This area examines the neural systems and physiological dynamics that support attentional focus, attentional lapses, and control over thought and behavior.



EEG Microstates and Mind Wandering
Investigating how EEG microstate dynamics track attentional state, time-on-task effects, and mind wandering during sustained performance.

Ongoing EEG Time-on-Task



Functional Network Dynamics
Studying how large-scale networks such as FPCN, DAN, and DMN interact to support attention control and internally directed cognition.

Ongoing fMRI Network Neuroscience



Pupillometry and LC-NE Function
Using pupil dynamics as an index of attentional engagement and locus coeruleus–norepinephrine system involvement in cognitive control.

Ongoing Pupillometry

title: Projects
text:
{#contemplative-training} Contemplative Training

This area explores whether and how contemplative practices such as mindfulness training can strengthen attention, reduce vulnerability to distraction, and support well-being in demanding contexts.



Acute Effects of Mindfulness Practice
Testing whether individual mindfulness practice sessions produce immediate changes in focus, mood, and stress.

Ongoing Repeated Measures Training Studies



Mindfulness Training in High-Demand Populations
Evaluating mindfulness-based interventions in groups such as firefighters and military personnel to understand training effects in applied settings.

Ongoing Intervention Applied Research



State-to-Trait Change in Attention and Well-Being
Studying whether repeated short-term shifts during contemplative practice accumulate into broader changes across training.

Ongoing Longitudinal Modeling
